title: Your first agent configuration with MCP and Looker
summary: Google Cloud video on building AI-powered agents using the Agent Development Kit and Looker, grounded in enterprise data. Covers Model Context Protocol, MCP Toolbox for Databases, tools.yaml configuration, and implementation through a Python environment with a local web interface.
writeup: A step-by-step walkthrough of connecting a local LLM agent to Looker via Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) and the MCP Toolbox for Databases. The build starts from nothing: Python virtual environment setup, Looker API credential retrieval, a tools.yaml file that declares which Looker capabilities the agent can call, and a root agent defined in Python. The central insight is that the tools.yaml description field is what the LLM reads to decide which tool to invoke, so a well-described tool definition directly shapes response quality.

key_points: Google ADK (Agent Development Kit) is an open-source framework built and proven at Google scale; it provides the scaffolding (ADK create, ADK web) for standing up a local agent with minimal boilerplate.

Looker authentication requires a client ID and client secret obtained from the user's own Looker account credentials; both must be stored as environment variables before any MCP connection is attempted.

Tools are declared in a tools.yaml file under a sources key (kind: looker, base URL, credential references) and a tools key where each entry names the Looker-specific functionality, for example get_connections, with a description the LLM uses to select the right tool at runtime.
The MCP Toolbox for Databases acts as the local MCP server; the agent Python file imports MCP toolsets and server parameters to wire the agent's root LLM to those declared tools.

Adding a tool is a live-reload operation: modifying tools.yaml and restarting ADK web immediately makes the new tool visible when queried, demonstrating the declarative, config-driven nature of the tooling layer.

Beyond get_connections, the Looker MCP toolset supports getting dashboards, Looks, Explores, toggling dev mode, and running health analysis, giving the agent broad read and inspection coverage of a Looker instance.
